card.indications: Treatment of nonobstructive urinary retention due to neurogenic bladder or postoperative/postpartum bladder atony.
card.mechanism: Directly stimulates muscarinic receptors, increasing detrusor tone and promoting bladder emptying.
card.dosing: Oral dosing is individualized, commonly 10 to 50 mg three or four times daily. Subcutaneous dosing is used less often and must be given carefully.
card.routeAdministration: Give on an empty stomach, one hour before or two hours after meals, to reduce nausea and vomiting. Never administer IM or IV.
card.contraindications: Mechanical GI or urinary obstruction, peptic ulcer, asthma, bradycardia, hypotension, coronary disease, epilepsy, hyperthyroidism, or recent GI/bladder surgery where contraction is unsafe.
card.warnings: Excess cholinergic activity can cause severe bradycardia, hypotension, bronchospasm, diarrhea, sweating, and abdominal cramping.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Abdominal cramps, diarrhea, nausea, salivation, flushing, sweating, urinary urgency, headache, and dizziness.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Bronchospasm, severe hypotension, bradycardia, syncope, cholinergic crisis, and worsening obstruction.
card.interactions: Anticholinergic drugs reduce effect. Cholinesterase inhibitors increase cholinergic toxicity. Ganglion blockers may cause severe hypotension.
card.monitoring: Assess bladder distention, voided volume, postvoid residual, blood pressure, pulse, respiratory status, abdominal symptoms, and signs of obstruction.
card.patientTeaching: Take on an empty stomach. Rise slowly. Report wheezing, fainting, severe abdominal pain, inability to urinate, or persistent diarrhea.
card.nutrition: Take one hour before or two hours after meals. Maintain hydration unless restricted.

card.indications: Short-term relief of occasional constipation and bowel preparation when directed.
card.mechanism: Stimulates enteric nerves and promotes colonic motility and fluid accumulation.
card.dosing: Adults and children 12 years and older commonly take 5 to 15 mg orally once daily for occasional constipation; rectal dosing is product specific.
card.routeAdministration: Swallow delayed-release tablets whole. Do not take within one hour of milk or antacids. Rectal products are for rectal use only.
card.contraindications: Known bowel obstruction, acute surgical abdomen, severe dehydration, or hypersensitivity. Seek evaluation for unexplained abdominal pain, nausea, or vomiting.
card.warnings: Overuse can cause fluid and electrolyte imbalance, laxative dependence, and impaired bowel function. Do not use longer than one week unless directed.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Abdominal cramps, diarrhea, nausea, rectal irritation, and urgency.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe dehydration, hypokalemia, syncope, ischemic colitis, and bowel injury from improper rectal use.
card.interactions: Antacids and milk can dissolve enteric coating early. Other drugs may be affected by rapid transit or electrolyte loss.
card.monitoring: Monitor bowel pattern, abdominal pain, hydration, electrolytes with repeated use, and signs of obstruction or GI bleeding.
card.patientTeaching: Use only short term. Swallow tablets whole and avoid milk or antacids within one hour. Report rectal bleeding, severe pain, or no bowel movement after use.
card.nutrition: Increase fluids, fiber, and activity when appropriate. Avoid using laxatives as a substitute for a balanced bowel routine.

card.indications: Short-term relief of diarrhea, upset stomach, heartburn, indigestion, and nausea; also used in selected H. pylori regimens.
card.mechanism: Coats irritated mucosa, binds enterotoxins, reduces intestinal secretion and inflammation, and has antimicrobial activity.
card.dosing: OTC dosing is product specific and may be repeated at labeled intervals up to the maximum daily dose. Pediatric use requires caution because of salicylate content.
card.routeAdministration: Shake liquid well and measure accurately. Chew tablets completely. Do not exceed label duration or dose.
card.contraindications: Salicylate allergy, active significant bleeding, children or adolescents recovering from viral illness, and use with certain anticoagulants without clinician guidance.
card.warnings: Can cause salicylate toxicity and bleeding. Children and adolescents with viral illness are at risk for Reye syndrome. Persistent diarrhea may signal serious disease.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Temporary darkening of tongue or stool, constipation, nausea, and mild abdominal discomfort.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Salicylate toxicity, serious bleeding, encephalopathy, severe allergic reaction, and Reye syndrome.
card.interactions: Anticoagulants, antiplatelets, aspirin, methotrexate, and other salicylates increase bleeding or toxicity. It can reduce absorption of tetracyclines.
card.monitoring: Monitor stool frequency and character, hydration, fever, blood in stool, tinnitus, confusion, bleeding, and duration of symptoms.
card.patientTeaching: Dark stool or tongue is usually harmless, but report bloody or tarry stool with weakness, ringing in ears, confusion, fever, or diarrhea lasting over two days.
card.nutrition: Maintain oral fluids and electrolytes during diarrhea. Avoid alcohol and additional salicylate-containing products.

card.indications: Treatment of hyperprolactinemia, acromegaly, Parkinson disease, and selected type 2 diabetes products.
card.mechanism: Stimulates dopamine D2 receptors, suppressing prolactin and growth hormone release and improving dopaminergic activity.
card.dosing: Dose and timing vary greatly by indication and formulation. Start low and titrate gradually to reduce nausea and hypotension.
card.routeAdministration: Take with food. Diabetes formulations have specific morning timing. Do not interchange products without guidance.
card.contraindications: Uncontrolled hypertension, hypersensitivity to ergot alkaloids, and certain postpartum uses. Contraindications vary by indication and product.
card.warnings: May cause severe hypotension, syncope, hallucinations, impulse-control disorders, pleural or retroperitoneal fibrosis, vasospasm, and sudden sleep.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, dizziness, headache, fatigue, constipation, nasal congestion, and orthostatic hypotension.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Stroke, myocardial infarction, severe hypertension, arrhythmias, psychosis, fibrosis, GI bleeding, and sudden sleep attacks.
card.interactions: Antipsychotics and metoclopramide reduce effect. CYP3A4 inhibitors increase exposure. Antihypertensives and alcohol worsen hypotension.
card.monitoring: Monitor indication-specific response, blood pressure, mental status, cardiac symptoms, pulmonary symptoms, glucose for diabetes use, and prolactin or growth hormone as ordered.
card.patientTeaching: Take with food and rise slowly. Report chest pain, severe headache, vision change, shortness of breath, hallucinations, compulsive behavior, or fainting.
card.nutrition: Take with food to reduce nausea. Avoid excessive alcohol. Diabetes patients should follow their meal and glucose plan closely.

card.indications: Maintenance treatment of asthma, allergic rhinitis, Crohn disease, ulcerative colitis, and other formulation-specific inflammatory conditions.
card.mechanism: Activates glucocorticoid receptors and suppresses inflammatory cytokines, edema, and immune activity. Extensive first-pass metabolism reduces but does not eliminate systemic effects.
card.dosing: Dosing depends entirely on formulation and indication. Use the lowest effective dose and taper systemic oral therapy when clinically required.
card.routeAdministration: Rinse mouth after inhalation. Prime inhalers or sprays as directed. Swallow oral delayed-release or extended-release products whole unless labeling says otherwise.
card.contraindications: Severe hypersensitivity. Inhaled products are not rescue treatment for acute bronchospasm. Live vaccines may be unsuitable with immunosuppressive doses.
card.warnings: Long-term use can cause adrenal suppression, infection, reduced bone density, cataracts, glaucoma, growth suppression, and hyperglycemia.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Hoarseness, oral thrush, cough, nasal irritation, headache, nausea, abdominal pain, and acne.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Adrenal crisis, severe infection, paradoxical bronchospasm, serious hypersensitivity, osteoporosis, cataracts, glaucoma, and psychiatric effects.
card.interactions: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors and grapefruit can raise systemic exposure. Other immunosuppressants increase infection risk.
card.monitoring: Monitor symptom control, rescue-inhaler use, oral cavity, growth in children, adrenal function when indicated, glucose, bone health, eyes, infection, and GI symptoms.
card.patientTeaching: Use every day as directed, not for sudden asthma attacks. Rinse and spit after inhalation. Report white mouth patches, vision changes, infection, severe weakness, or worsening breathing.
card.nutrition: Oral products may have food-specific instructions. Avoid grapefruit when directed. Ensure adequate calcium and vitamin D with prolonged therapy.
